scenarios.name: Coffee Shop
scenarios.description: Order coffee and cafe drinks easily
scenarios.tip: Spain coffee stops often happen at a bar or cafe counter, so keep the order short and be ready to ask for the bill or takeaway.

scenarios.name: Street Food / Restaurant
scenarios.tip: For Spain, treat this as bar, tapas, and casual restaurant language; water, takeaway, and the bill matter more than bargaining or extra customization.

scenarios.name: Ride App / Taxi
scenarios.tip: Showing the destination on your phone still helps, especially for airport pickups, hotels, and old-town drop-offs.

scenarios.name: Asking the Price
scenarios.tip: In Spain, use this mostly for price confirmation and item choice; marked prices are not a haggling lane.

scenarios.name: Polite Basics
scenarios.tip: Excuse-me and thank-you language carries more traveler value than long introductions, especially in busy service interactions.

scenarios.name: Convenience Store
scenarios.tip: Use this for kiosks, small shops, and quick pharmacy-style asks where card payment, receipts, and water are frequent needs.

scenarios.name: Hotel / Hostel
scenarios.tip: Keep your passport and booking details ready because front desks often move quickly and reservation confirmation solves a lot of friction.

scenarios.name: Directions
scenarios.tip: Save your hotel, station, or metro stop on your phone because Spain-friendly direction help is often about stations, platforms, or whether something is walkable.

scenarios.name: Simple Problems
scenarios.tip: If something goes wrong, stay calm and show booking screens, tickets, or screenshots to speed up help.

scenarios.name: Small Talk
scenarios.tip: Keep this light and optional; practical travel utility should be authored before conversation filler.

notes: Shared traveler baseline scaffold for Spain-focused Spanish, now reprioritized toward cafes, transport, payment, and repair language before runtime wiring.
